title: 第 16 節
en: ‘And Kitra said: All who depart from this world (or this body) go to the moon. In the former, (the waxing) half, the moon waxes big by their vital spirits, but in the other, (the waning) half, the moon causes them to be born. Verily, the moon is the door of the Svarga-world (heavenly world). Now, if aman answer the moon (rightly)*, the moon sets him free. But if a man does not answer the moon, the moon showers him down, having become rain, upon this earth. And according to his deeds, and according to his knowledge, he is born again here as a worm, or as an insect, or as a fish, or as a bird, or as a lion, or as a boar, or as a serpent (?), or as a tiger, or as a man, or 1 See Bloomfield, Journal of the American Oriental Society, vol. xv. p. 168; Boehtlingk, Khandogya-Upanishad, p. 92.
2 Cf. Boehtlingk, Uber eine bisher arg missverstandene Stelle in der Kaushitaki-Brahmana-Upanishad.

JOURNEY OF THE SOUL AFTER DEATH. 121 as somebody else in different places. But when he has arrived, the moon asks him: “ Who art thou? ” And he shall answer: “O seasons!, the seed was brought from the bright moon who was poured forth (in rain) ; who consists of fifteen parts, who harbours our fathers? ; raise me now 1Π a vigorous man, and pour me through a vigorous man into a mother.

‘«Then I am born as the twelfth or thirteenth additional month through the twelveor thirteen-fold father (the year). I know that, I remember that. O seasons, bring me then to immortality. By this truth and by this penance I am a season ὃ, a child of the seasons. I am thou.” Thereupon the moon sets him free.

‘Having reached the Path of the gods, he comes to the world of Agni (fire), to the world of Vayu (air), to the world of Varuna, to the world of Indra, to the world of PragApati, to the world of Brahman. In that world there is the lake Ara, the moments called Yeshtiha, the river Vigara (ageless), the tree Ilya, the city Sdlagya, the palace Aparagita (unconquerable), the door-keepers Indra and Pragapati, the hall of Brahman, called Vibhu, the throne Vikakshana (intelligence), the couch Amitaugas (endless splendour), and the beloved Manast (mind), and her image Kakshushi (eye), who, taking flowers, are weaving the worlds, and the Apsaras, the Ambas (scriptures 7), and Ambayavis (understanding ?), and the rivers Ambayas. To this world he who knows this approaches. Brahman 1 The seasons are sometimes called the brothers of Soma, the moon.
? When only the fifteenth part is left of the moon, the Pitris enter it. Ludwig takes the Ribhus also for the genii of the seasons.
3 The seasons are parts of the lunar year that seem to come and go like the lives of mortal men.
says, “ Run towards him with such worship as is due to myself. He has reached the river Vigara (ageless), he will never age.”

‘Then five hundred Apsaras go towards him, one hundred with fruit in their hands, one hundred with ointments in their hands, one hundred with garlands in their hands, one hundred with garments in their hands, one hundred with perfumes in their hands. They adorn him with an adornment worthy of Brahman, and when thus adorned with the adornment of Brahman, the knower of Brahman moves towards Brahman. He (the departed) approaches the lake Ara. and crosses it by the mind, while those who come to it without knowing the truth, are drowned init. He comes to the moments called Yeshtiha, and they flee from him. He comes to the river Vigara, and crosses it by the mind alone, and then shakes off his good and evil deeds!. His beloved relatives obtain the good, his unbeloved relatives the evil he has done. And as a man driving in a chariot, might look at the two wheels, thus he will look at day and night, thus at good and evil deeds, and at all pairs (correlative things). Being freed from good and evil he, the knower of Brahman, moves towards Brahman.

‘He approaches the tree Ilya, and the odour of Brahman reaches him. He approaches the city Salagya, and the flavour of Prahman reaches him. He approaches the palace Aparagita, and the splendour of Brahman reaches him. He approaches the door-keepers Indra and Pragapati, and they run away from him. He approaches the hall Vibhu, and the 1 Cf, Knand. Up. VIII. 18.

JOURNEY OF THE SOUL AFTER DEATH. 123 glory of Brahman reaches him. He approaches the throne Vikakshand. The Sdman verses, Brihat and Rathantara, are the eastern feet of that throne; the Saman verses, Syaita and Naudhasa, its western feet ; the Saman verses, Vairiipa and Vairdga, its sides, lengthways ; the Saman verses, SAkvara and Raivata, its sides, crossways. That throne is Prag/iad (knowledge), for by knowledge he sees clearly. He approaches the couch Amitaugas. That is prdna (breath, speech). The past and the future are its eastern feet ; prosperity and earth its western feet; the Saman verses, Brihat and Rathantara, are the two sides lengthways of the couch; the Sdman verses, Bhadra and Yagiiayagiiya, are the cross-sides at the head and feet (east and west); the Rik and Saman are the long sheets, the Yagus the cross-sheets, the moonbeams the cushion, the Udgitha the coverlet; prosperity the pillow. On this couch sits Brahman, and he who knows this, mounts it first with one foot. Then Brahman says to him: “ Who art thou?” and he shall answer: “I am a season, and the child of the seasons, sprung from the womb of endless space, the seed of the wife, the light of the year, the self of all that is. Thou art the self of all that is; what thou art, that am J.” ’
Difficulties of Interpretation.

This is as close a translation as I can give. But I must confess that many of the names here used in describing the reception given by the god Brahman to the departed, are unintelligible to me. They were equally unintelligible to the native commentators, who, however, try to discover a meaning in some of them, 124 LECTURE V.

as when they explain the lake Ara, which the departed has to cross, as derived from Ari, enemy, these enemies being the passions and inclinations of the heart. We are told afterwards that those who come to that lake without knowing the truth, are drowned in it. When the throne, on which Brahman is seated, is called Vikakshana, this seems to mean Intelligence, and Manasi also is probably a personification of the mind of which Kakshushi, representing the eye, may well be called the image. But there is such a mixture of symbolical and purely picturesque language in all this, and the text seems so often quite corrupt, that it seems hopeless to discover the original intention of the poet, whoever he was, that first imagined this meeting between the departed and the god Brahman. On some points we gain a little light, as, for instance, when we are told that the departed, after having crossed the river Vigara (the ageless) by his mind, shakes off his good and his evil deeds, and that he leaves the benefit of his good deeds to those among his relatives who are dear to him, while his evil deeds fall to the share of his unbeloved relations. We also see more clearly that the throne on which Brahman sits is meant for Praga or wisdom, while the couch Amitaugas is identified with prana, that is breath and speech, and the coverings with the Vedas.

Though there is a general likeness in these different accounts of the fate of the soul after death, still we see how each Upanishad has something peculiar to say on the subject. In some the subject is treated very briefly, as in the Mundaka-Upanishad I. 2, 11, where we are only told that the soul of the pious man passes through the gate of the sun where the immortal Person JOURNEY OF THE SOUL AFTER DEATH. 15)

(spirit) dwells. In the Ahandogya-Upanishad VIII. 6, 5, one account is equally brief. Here we are told that the soul departs upwards by the rays of the sun, reaches the sun, which is the door to the worlds (loka) for the wise, but a bar to the foolish. The Brihadaranyaka also gives in one passage (V.10, 1) a short account of the soul’s journey from the body to the air, from the air to the sun, from the sun to the moon, from the moon to the painless world where the soul dwells for eternal years. Similar short accounts occur in Taitt. Up. I. 6, and Prasna Up. I. 9.
Historical Progress in the Upanishads.

If we look at the fuller accounts, we can easily perceive that the earliest conception of life after death was that represented by the PitriyAna, the Path of the Fathers, that is, the path which led the soul to the moon, where the Fathers, or those who have gone before him, dwell. The description of this path is much the same in the Brihad-aranyaka and in the KhandogyaUpanishad. The soul enters into smoke (probably of the funeral pile), then comes to the night, then to the waning half of the moon, then to the six months when the sun moves towards the South. But it does not reach the year, but moves straight to the abode of the Fathers and to the moon. When this abode in the moon came to be considered as temporary only, and as followed by a new cycle of existences, it was natural to imagine a DevayAna which led beyond to the gods and to eternal happiness without any return to new transmigrations. But this abode in the Devaloka also did not satisfy all desires, and a further progress was admitted from the sun to the moon, or 126 LECTURE V.

direct from the sun to the abode of lightning, from whence a spirit led the souls to the world of Brahman. This world, though still conceived in mythological phraseology, was probably for a long time the highest point reached by the thinkers and poets of the Upanishads, but we shall see that after a time even this approach to a personal and objective God was not considered final, and that there was a higher bliss which could be reached by knowledge only, or by the consciousness of the soul’s inseparateness from Brahman. We see traces of this in passages of the Upanishads such as Brih. Ar. Up. V. 4, 8, ‘ Wise people who know Brahman go on this road (devayana) to the heaven-world (svarga), and higher up from thence, as quite freed” Or Maitr. Brahm. Up. VI. 30, ‘Stepping over the world of Brahman, they go by it to the highest path.’

While to our minds the belief in the soul’s journey to the world of the Fathers, the world of the gods, and the world of the mythological Brahman (masc.), seems to present an historical development, it was not so with Vedanta philosophers. They looked upon every passage in the Upanishads as equally true, because revealed, and they tried to combine all the accounts of the soul’s journey, even when they clearly differed from one another, into one harmonious whole.
Attempts to harmonise the different Statements of the Upanishads.

How they achieved this, I shall best be able to show you by translating some portion of the Vedantasitras with the commentary by Sankara. Though some of it may seem tedious, yet it will be useful in JOURNEY OF THE SOUL AFTER DEATH. 197 giving you some idea of the style and spirit of the later Vedanta philosophers. You will observe how the Sitras by themselves are almost unintelligible, though we sce, after reading Sankara’s comments, that they really contain the gist of the whole argument.
First Sorra.
On the road beginning with light, c., because this is widely recognised.

Sankara explains: From the beginning of the journey (of the departed) the process, as stated, is the same. But the actual journey is revealed differently in different sacred texts. One, by means of the junction of the arteries with the solar rays, is found in the Khand. Up. VIII. 6, 5, ‘Then he mounts upwards by those very rays. Another, beginning with the light (arkis) is found in Khand. Up. V. 10, 1, ‘They go to the light, from light to day.’ Another occurs in the Kaush. Up. I. 3, ‘Having reached the path of the gods, he comes to the world of Agni, or fire.” Again, another occurs in the Brih. Ar. V. 10, 1, ‘ When the person goes away from this world, he comes to the wind. And one more in the Mund. Up. I. 2, 11, says, ‘They depart free from passions through the gate of the sun.’

Here then a doubt arises, whether these roadsare really different from each other, or whether it is one and the same road, only differently described. It is assumed, by way of argument, that they are different roads, be- 128 LECTURE V.

cause they occur inthe Upanishads under different heads and belong to different kinds of religious meditation (upasana); also because the limitation that he mounts upward by these very rays, would be contradicted, if we regarded what is said about light (ariis) and the rest ; and the statement about the quickness, when it it said, ‘as quickly as he sends off the mind 4, he goes to the sun,’ would also be upset. If on these grounds it is said that these roads are different from one another, we reply: No, ‘On the road beginning with light ;’ that is, We answer that every one who desires Brahman, hastens on by the road that begins with the light. And why ?—JBecause that road is so widely recognised. For that road is known indeed to all sages. Thus it is said in the chapter on the Five Fires, ‘And those also, who in the forest worship the True (i.e. Brahman) as faith, &c., clearly proclaiming that this road beginning with the light, is meant for those also who practise other kinds of knowledge. This might pass, we are told, and with regard to those kinds of knowledge for which no road whatever is mentioned, the road beginning with the light might be admitted. But if another and another road are proclaimed, why should the road beginning with the light be accepted? Our answer to all this is simply this. This might be so, if these roads were entirely different, but it is really one and the same road with different 1 The words sa yavat kshipyen manas tivat are difficult to translate. They are meant to express quickness (kshipratvam from kship), wind, mind, and horse being the general representa: tions of quickne 55: ἢ had translated formerly, and ‘while his mind is failing,’ which Boehtlingk should not have adopted, rendering it by ‘ Wahrend das De nkorgan verschwindet’ ; but it is clear that quickness, and not fainting, was intended, and it was so understood by the author of the Vedanta-sitras.

time JOURNEY OF THE SOUL AFTER DEATH. 129 attributes, leading to the world of Brahman, and sometimes determined by one, sometimes by another predicate. For whenever one part has been recognised, the relation should be that as between what determines and what is to be determined 1, and the various determinations of the road must be summed up together, just as we sum up the several attributes of a science which is one and the same, though its treatments may vary. And even if the subject (under which a certain road to Brahman is taught) is different, the road is the same, because its goal is the same, and because one part of the road has been recognised (as the same). For in all the following passages one and the same object, viz. the obtainment of the Brahma-world, is clearly shown. We read (Brith. Art Ni ὃ; 15): ln these worlds of Brahman they dwell for ever and ever ;’—(Brih. V. 10, 1): ‘There he dwells eternal years; tense LO):

‘Whatever victory, whatever greatness belongs to Brahman, that victory he gives, that greatness he reaches ; —(A/and. VIII. 4,3): ‘That world of Brahman belongs to those only who find it by Brahmakarya.’ And if itis said that in admitting the approach to the light, there would be no room for the restriction expressed in the words, ‘ By these very rays,’ that is no fault ; for its true object is the reaching of these rays. The same word which includes the obtainment of the rays, need not exclude the light, &c. Therefore we must admit that this very union with the rays is here emphasised. And what is said about the speed is the whole. But the translation is unsatisfactory, nor does Professor Deussen make the drift of the sentence clearer, The ekadesa here is simply meant for the beginning and the end of the road.
not upset, if we confine ourselves to the road beginning with light, for the object is quickness, as if it were said, one gets there in the twinkling of an eye.
zh: 『奇特羅說：凡離開此世界（或此身體）者皆往月亮。在前者（盈月）半月，月亮因其生命精氣而漸盈滿；但在另一個（虧月）半月，月亮使他們得以投生。誠然，月亮乃是天界世界（Svarga-world）之門。今若一人正確地答應月亮*，月亮則放他自由。但若一人未能答應月亮，月亮則化為雨降落此地球之上。按其所為之業行，按其所具之知識，他便再生於此世為蟲、為蟲豸、為魚、為鳥、為獅、為豬、為蛇（？）、為虎、為人，或¹ ¹ 參見 Bloomfield, Journal of the American Oriental Society, vol. xv. p. 168; Boehtlingk, Khandogya-Upanishad, p. 92.』
2 參見博特林克，《論《考希塔基梵書奧義書》中一個迄今遭到嚴重誤解的段落》(Boehtlingk, Über eine bisher arg missverstandene Stelle in der Kaushitaki-Brahmana-Upanishad)。
死後靈魂的旅程。121 作為他人在不同的地方。但當他到達時，月亮問他：「汝為何人？」而他應當回答：「季節啊！種子是從光輝的月亮帶來的，那月亮曾被傾注而下（如雨）；它由十五個部分組成，它容納我們的祖先；現在請提升我為一個強健的人，並通過一個強健的人將我注入一個母親。
「那麼，我就作為第十二個或第十三個附加的月份而被生成，透過十二倍或十三倍的父親（即年份）。我知道這一點，我記得這一點。哦季節啊，那麼就請把我帶往不朽之境。由於這真理和這苦行，我就是一個季節 ὃ，是季節的孩子。我就是你。」由此，月亮便將他解放了。

「已抵達諸神之路，他來到阿格尼（火）的世界，來到瓦尤（風）的世界，來到瓦魯納的世界，來到因陀羅的世界，來到普拉賈帕蒂的世界，來到梵天的世界。在那世界中有阿羅湖，名為耶什蒂哈的時刻，維迦羅河（不老的），伊利亞樹，斯達拉吉亞城，阿帕拉吉塔宮（不可征服的），門衛因陀羅與普拉賈帕蒂，名為毘卜的梵天大廳，維迦克沙納寶座（智慧），阿密陶伽斯榻（無盡的光耀），以及所愛的曼那斯特（心），及其化身卡克舒希（眼），她們採花編織世界，及仙女（Apsaras）、安巴斯（經典），及安巴亞維斯（理解？），及安巴亞斯河。凡知此者，即趨近於此世界。梵天。季節有時被稱為蘇摩（月）的兄弟。」
當月亮僅餘十五分之一時，彼得里斯（Pitris）進入其中。路德維希也將力不斯（Ribhus）視為季節的精靈。
季節是太陰曆年的組成部分，它們的來去似如凡人的生命那般變幻無常。
說：「懷著如同對我本身應有的敬禮朝向他奔去。他已抵達維嘉拉河（Vigara，無衰老者），他將永不衰老。」

「那時五百位阿普薩拉斯（Apsaras）走向他，一百位手中持著果實，一百位手中持著油膏，一百位手中持著花環，一百位手中持著衣袍，一百位手中持著香料。他們以梵天所配得的莊嚴來裝飾他，當如此以梵天的莊嚴所裝飾之後，梵天的知者朝向梵天而行。他（已逝者）接近阿羅湖（Ara），並以心念越過它，而那些來到此湖卻不知真理者，則溺水其中。他來到名為葉什底哈（Yeshtiha）的時刻，而它們在他面前逃離。他來到薇嘎羅河，並以心念單獨越過它，之後便脫卸了他的善行與惡行。他所鍾愛的親戚獲得了他所為的善行，他所不愛的親戚獲得了他所為的惡行。猶如一個駕駛戰車的人能觀看兩個輪子那樣，他將如此觀看晝夜，如此觀看善行與惡行，以及所有的對偶（相關連的事物）。既已從善與惡中得到解脫，梵天的知者便朝向梵天而行。」
「他靠近伊利亞樹木，梵天的氣息傳達於他。他靠近薩拉吉亞城，梵天的風味傳達於他。他靠近阿帕拉吉塔宮殿，梵天的光輝傳達於他。他靠近守門人因陀羅和波羅闍波提，他們從他身邊逃遁。他靠近毘婆殿堂，而1 參見《凱那陀奧義書》VIII. 18。」

榮耀歸於梵天者到達他。他接近王座維迦克善德（Vikakshand）。讚美詩 Brihat 與 Rathantara 是該王座的東方腳；讚美詩 Syaita 與 Naudhasa 是其西方腳；讚美詩 Vairiipa 與 Vairdga 是其長邊；讚美詩 SAkvara 與 Raivata 是其寬邊。該王座為普若智（Pragnad，知識），蓋以知識而能明見。他接近寢台阿彌陶葛斯（Amitaugas）。此為普若那（prāṇa，呼吸‧語言）。過去與未來為其東方腳；繁榮與大地為其西方腳；讚美詩 Brihat 與 Rathantara 為寢台長邊的兩側；讚美詩 Bhadra 與 Yagiiayagiiya 為頭腳處的寬邊（東與西）；梨俱經與讚美詩為縱向床單，雅柔經為橫向床單，月光為靠墊，烏德吉它為被套；繁榮為枕頭。在此寢台上坐著梵天，而知此者，首先以一隻腳踏上它。於是梵天對他說：「汝是誰？」而他應答：「我是季節，季節之子，生於無盡虛空之子宮，妻子之種子，年之光，萬物之自我。汝乃萬物之自我；汝之所是，即我之所是。」

然而，即便我們承認，在宗教學中，我們必須處理的現象，其本質與語言科學所處理的現象截然不同，我們仍不可忘記，在宗教學中，如同在語言科學中，我們所擁有的，不過是些殘篇斷簡。我們所擁有的宗教文獻，沒有一部是完整的。我們所擁有的吠陀（Veda），不過是古代吠陀詩人吟唱詩歌的殘餘選集。我們所擁有的《阿維斯陀》（Avesta），不過是瑣羅亞斯德教（Zoroaster）經典的一小部分。我們所擁有的佛教《三藏》（Tripitaka），不過是佛陀教誨的殘篇。我們所擁有的《摩西五經》（Pentateuch），不過是古代律法與歷史的殘篇。我們所擁有的《新約》（New Testament），不過是基督教早期文獻的選集。我們所擁有的《古蘭經》（Koran），不過是穆罕默德（Mohammed）啟示的殘篇。我們所擁有的《奧義書》（Upanishads），不過是古代印度哲學的殘篇。我們所擁有的《埃達》（Edda），不過是古代北歐神話的殘篇。我們所擁有的《荷馬史詩》（Homer），不過是古代希臘宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《聖經》（Bible），不過是古代希伯來宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《古蘭經》，不過是古代阿拉伯宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《吠陀》，不過是古代雅利安（Aryan）宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《阿維斯陀》，不過是古代波斯宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《三藏》，不過是古代佛教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《奧義書》，不過是古代印度哲學的殘篇。我們所擁有的《埃達》，不過是古代北歐神話的殘篇。我們所擁有的《荷馬史詩》，不過是古代希臘宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《聖經》，不過是古代希伯來宗教的殘篇。我們所擁有的《古蘭經》，不過是古代阿拉伯宗教的殘篇。
這是我所能提供最貼切的譯文。但我必須坦白，許多用於描述提婆梵天（god Brahman）對亡者款待的名詞對我而言是難以理解的。這些名詞對於本地評論家同樣難以理解，不過他們試圖在其中某些名詞中發掘意義。

正如他們解釋離世者必須渡過的湖泊阿拉（Ara）時，將其衍生自「敵人」（Ari），這些敵人代表心中的激情與傾向。我們隨後被告知，那些未知真理而來到該湖的人會在其中溺水。當梵天（Brahman）所坐的寶座被稱為毗迦刹那（Vikakshana）時，這似乎意味著智慧；摩那西（Manasi）也可能是心的擬人化，而代表眼睛的迦刹舒希（Kakshushi）可以被恰當地稱為其形象。但在所有這些當中，象徵性與純粹具象性的語言混雜在一起，文本似乎往往相當訛誤，因此要發現最初想像離世者與梵天神相遇的詩人的原始意圖似乎是無望的。在某些方面我們獲得了些許啟發，例如當我們被告知離世者在用心念渡過無衰河（Vigara）之後，便擺脫其善業與惡業，他將善業的福報留給他所摯愛的親戚，而他的惡業則歸屬於他不摯愛的親戚。我們也更清楚地看出，梵天所坐的寶座是為了普拉迦（Praga）或智慧，而卧榻阿密陶伽斯（Amitaugas）則與普拉那（prana）相同，亦即呼吸與言語，而其覆蓋物與吠陀相應。
儘管這些關於靈魂死後命運的各種說法存在普遍的相似性，我們仍然可以看出每一部奧義書在這個主題上都有其獨特之處。在某些奧義書中，這個主題的論述非常簡略，如《蒙德卡奧義書》I. 2, 11 中，我們僅被告知虔誠人的靈魂通過太陽之門，在那裡永恆的人格 《靈魂死後的旅程》。15)

（精神）所居之處。在《阿含多基亞奧義書》（Ahandogya-Upanishad）VIII. 6, 5 中，有另一個同樣簡要的記述。我們在此被告知靈魂沿著日光的光芒向上出發，抵達太陽，太陽對於智者而言是通往諸世界（loka）的門戶，但對於愚昧者而言卻是障礙。《布里哈達蘭亞卡奧義書》（Brihadaranyaka）在一處經文（V.10, 1）中也給出了靈魂從身體到空氣、從空氣到太陽、從太陽到月亮、從月亮到無苦世界的短暫旅程的記述，靈魂在該世界永恆的歲月中居留。類似的簡短記述同樣出現在《泰特尼亞奧義書》（Taitt. Up.）I. 6 和《普拉什那奧義書》（Prasna Up.）I. 9 中。
奧義書中的歷史進步

如果我們查閱更詳盡的記載，便不難看出死後生命的最早觀念乃是由毗多利耶那（Pitriyāna）所代表的，即「父祖之路」，亦即引導靈魂往月亮的道路，諸父祖或先於他而去者皆居於彼處。此路的描述在《廣林奧義書》（Brihad-aranyaka）與《歌者奧義書》（Khandogya Upanishad）中大致相同。靈魂進入煙霧中（大概是喪禮火堆的煙），隨後來到夜晚，再到月亮的虧缺半月，然後到達太陽向南移動的六個月。但它並未到達一年之終，而是直接進入父祖的住所與月亮。當月亮中的此住所開始被視為僅是暫時的，並且隨之而來的是新的輪迴週期時，自然就會想像存在著提婆耶那（Devayāna），它引領靈魂超越月亮而至諸神與永恆幸福之地，無須再次投生轉世。然而提婆羅迦（Devaloka）中的此住所亦未能滿足所有的欲望，人們認可靈魂進一步從太陽至月亮的進展，或更進一步。

直接從太陽前往閃電的居所，從那裡一位靈魂引導者將靈魂帶往梵天的世界。這個世界儘管仍以神話的措辭來構想，但在很長一段時間裡，它可能代表了奧義書思想家和詩人所達到的最高境界，然而我們將看到，時間一久，即使這種對具格的、客觀的神的趨近也不再被視為最終的，而且存在著一種更高的至樂，唯有透過知識才能到達，或透過靈魂與梵天不可分離性的覺知才能到達。我們在奧義書的若干段落中可以看到這種跡象，例如《白馬奧義書》(Brih. Ar. Up.) 第五卷第四至八節：「知梵天的智者沿著這條路（devayana）前往天界世界（svarga），並從那裡更高處前進，如同完全解脫者」；或《美特雷奧義書》(Maitr. Brahm. Up.) 第六卷第三十節：「超越梵天世界而行，他們由此前往最高之路。」
雖然在我們的理解中，靈魂前往祖先世界、諸神世界，以及神話式梵天（masc.）世界的信念似乎呈現出一種歷史發展的過程，但對於吠檀多哲學家來說並非如此。他們將《奧義書》中的每一段經文視為同等真實，因為這是啟示，並且他們試圖將所有關於靈魂旅程的記載融合為一個和諧的整體，即使這些記載彼此顯然相悖。
調和奧義書各種陳述的嘗試
我將最好地透過翻譯《韋檀多經》（Vedantasitras）的某些部分及商羯羅（Sankara）的註疏來向諸位展示他們如何達成此目的。儘管其中某些部分可能顯得冗長乏味，但它將有助於讓諸位瞭解後期韋檀多哲學家的風格與精神。諸位將會發現，這些《經》（Sitras）本身幾乎是難以理解的，然而在閱讀商羯羅的註疏後，我們會發現它們實際上包含了整個論證的精髓。
首先是悲痛。
在以光開始的道路上，§c.，因為這是廣為公認的。

商羯羅解釋道：從亡者旅程的開始，其過程如所述者相同。然而，實際的旅程在不同的聖典中以不同的方式啟示。其一透過動脈與日光線的匯合而見於《歌者奧義書》（Khand. Up.）第八篇第六章第五節：「然後他藉著那些光線向上升起。」另一種以光（arkis）開始的見於《歌者奧義書》第五篇第十章第一節：「他們去向光，從光到日。」又一種見於《考希多奧義書》（Kaush. Up.）第一篇第三章：「到達神的路徑後，他來到阿耆尼即火的世界。」復次，另一種見於《梵書叙說》（Brih. Ar.）第五篇第十章第一節：「當人離開此世界時，他來到風。」又有一種見於《蒙德卡奧義書》（Mund. Up.）第一篇第二章第十一節，言：「他們無罪惡地穿過太陽之門而離去。」
那麼在此產生了一個疑問：這些道路是否真的彼此不同，抑或它們實則為同一條道路，只是被不同地描述？作為論證的假設，我們假定它們是不同的道路，

因為它們在《奧義書》中出現於不同的標題下，且屬於不同類型的宗教冥想（upasana）；同時，因為「他藉由這些光線向上攀升」這一限制條件，如果我們將關於光（aris）及其他事項的陳述視為有效，就會被否定；而「迅速如心之所馳」這樣說法中關於迅速的陳述，也會被推翻。若基於這些理由說這些道路彼此不同，我們的回答是：否，「踏上始於光的道路」；也就是說，我們回答凡是渴望梵天者，皆急速踏上始於光的道路。為何如此？——因為那條道路被廣泛認可。因為那條道路確實為所有聖者所知。正如在《五火章》中所說的那樣，「以及那些在林中崇敬真理（即梵天）為信仰者等，也是如此」，明白表示這條始於光的道路，也是為那些修習其他種類知識者而設。有人告訴我們，這或可接受；至於那些沒有提及任何道路的知識類型，始於光的道路也許可以被認可。但倘若另有其他道路一再被宣示，為什麼應當接受始於光的道路呢？我們對所有這些詢問的回答甚為簡單。若這些道路完全不同，情況或許如此，但實際上它是同一條道路，只是有著不同的 1 「sa yavat kshipyen manas tivat」這些詞難以翻譯。它們意欲表達迅速（kshipratvam 源自 kship），風、心念與馬匹為迅速的通常代表。我從前曾譯為「當其心智衰微時」，波特林格不應採納此譯，將其譯為「當認知器官消失時」；但顯然意圖所在乃迅速而非昏迷，而《韋檀多經》的著者亦是如此理解的。

各種屬性導向梵天的世界，有時由某一謂詞所決定，有時由另一謂詞所決定。蓋凡一部分既已認識，其關係應如決定者與被決定者之間的關係 1，而通往梵天之路的各種決定必須彙總在一起，正如我們彙總同一門科學的若干屬性一般，儘管其論述形式有所不同。且即使教授某條通往梵天之路的主題（subject）各不相同，該路仍為同一，因其目標相同，且該路的某一部分已被認識（為相同）。蓋在下列所有段落中，同一個目的，即獲得梵天世界，均昭然若揭。我們讀到（Brith. Art Ni ὃ; 15）：「在梵天的這些世界中，他們永遠永遠地居住；」——（Brih. V. 10, 1）：「他在此居住永恆的年歲；」

「凡屬於梵天的勝利，凡屬於梵天的偉大，他都賦予那勝利，他都達致那偉大；」—（《奧義書》VIII. 4,3）：「梵天的世界只屬於那些通過梵行（Brahmakarya）發現它的人。」而若有人說，既然承認接近光明，在「以這些光線」所表達的限制中就沒有空間，那是無可厚非的；因為其真實目的乃在於達到這些光線。同一個詞包含了光線的獲得，不必排除光明，諸如此類。因此我們必須承認，正是這與光線的結合在此受到強調。而關於速度所說的乃是全部。但譯文並不令人滿意，德伊森教授（Professor Deussen）亦未能使句子的趨意更清楚。此處的一間隙（ekadesa）簡單地是指道路的開始與終點。
並不會感到困擾，如果我們將自己侷限在以光開始的道路上，因為目的在於快速，彷彿有人說，人在眨眼之間就能到達。
